the_one_thing.statement: Bar graphs with scale of 10 represent data where each unit on the axis equals 10, and students can use skip-counting by 10s to read and create these graphs, including values that fall between tick marks.

critical_misconception.label: Can't Interpolate Bar Heights
critical_misconception.why_critical: Students read only marked values on scaled axes. When a bar falls between 30 and 40 on an axis scaled by 10s, they report '30' or 'I don't know' instead of reading the halfway point as 35.

the_one_thing.success_indicator: Student correctly creates a bar graph where 35 items = bar ending between the 30 and 40 lines AND can explain '35 is between 30 and 40—halfway, because half of 10 is 5.'
the_one_thing.biggest_risk: Students who can't read values between tick marks will struggle with M5's scale selection (comparing efficiency across scales) and M6's multi-step problem-solving (which requires precise value reading).

student_facing_goal: You'll learn to make bar graphs where the scale is 10—each mark on the side shows 10 more. You already know how to count by 10s. That's the same skill you'll use here!
exit_check_tests: Can student create a scaled bar graph (1:10) from a data table?
Can student determine correct bar height for multiples of 10 (40 items = bar at 40 mark)?
Can student determine correct bar height for multiples of 5 that aren't multiples of 10 (35 items = bar halfway between 30 and 40)?
Can student explain the connection between skip-counting by 10s and scale?
Can student identify the axis and explain its function?
Can student answer 'how many more/fewer' and 'in all' questions using scaled bar graphs?
learning_goals: Represent data using scaled bar graphs.
Draw a scaled bar graph to represent a data set with several categories. Solve one- and two-step 'how many more' and 'how many fewer' problems using information presented in scaled bar graphs.

vocabulary_staging.approach: Use naturally; connect to M3: 'You made bar graphs with scale of 5. Now scale of 10!' No formal axis vocabulary yet.

Explicitly introduce after visual comparison: 'The numbers along the side are called the AXIS. The axis shows the scale. Read where the HEIGHT OF THE BAR ends.' Note: 'axis' is used naturally by Guide but students are not required to produce or define it—acceptable student language is 'the number line on the side,' 'where the numbers are.'
Explicitly teach during first interpolation task: 'This bar ends BETWEEN THE LINES—between 30 and 40. That's HALFWAY. Half of 10 is 5, so 30 + 5 = 35.'
Light touch: 'Bar graphs have two axes—one for categories, one for values.' Use when discussing both axes.
Use in question contexts; include 'What value does this show?' phrasing
Expect natural student use; consolidate axis vocabulary and scale progression pattern

value_range.lesson_section_2: 15-55 (multiples of 5, mix of at-line and between-line)
value_range.lesson_section_3: 20-60 (multiples of 5, mix of at-line and between-line)
value_range.exit_check: 20-60 (multiples of 5, mix of at-line and between-line)

building_toward: 3.MD.B.3 (full mastery — strategic scale selection and multi-step problem-solving, M5-M6)

bridge.from_module_3: Students learned to create BOTH picture graphs AND bar graphs with scale of 5. They understand skip-counting as the reading strategy and that bars show the same data as symbols ('same data, different format'). They are comfortable with both orientations. M3 Synthesis previewed: 'Next time: scale of 10.'
bridge.this_module: Students extend bar graph creation to scale of 10 through: bar graph as PRIMARY format (picture graphs appear only for transformation comparison); formal 'axis' vocabulary introduction; NEW interpolation — reading values BETWEEN axis lines (35 is halfway between 30 and 40); half-of-10 connection ('half of 10 = 5'); visual-to-numeric bridge (picture → bar graph same data).
bridge.to_module_5: Students will learn to CHOOSE the right scale for their data. M4 gives them a fourth scale in their toolkit (1, 2, 5, 10). M5 focuses on strategic decision-making. M4 does NOT include scale comparison or selection — students use scale of 10 exclusively.

core_concepts: Scale of 10: Each Axis Unit = 10 Items
Bar Graph as Primary Format (Picture Graphs Observation Only)
Interpolation: Reading Values Between Axis Lines
Scale Progression: 1 → 2 → 5 → 10 (More Efficient for Bigger Numbers)

available_visuals.description: Bar Graphs (Mode 2: Creating) are the PRIMARY visual for M4. Picture Graphs appear ONLY for transformation comparison in Warmup — students do NOT create picture graphs. Ghost gridlines at 5s (lighter than 10s lines) are essential for interpolation tasks. Snap-to-5 enabled in Sections 2-3; Snap-to-10 in Section 1. Bar interaction is click-to-set (hover shows preview, click confirms) — NOT drag. Data Tables use Numeric Display only throughout M4.

bar_graph_specs.ghost_gridlines: At 5s (lighter weight than 10s lines) — visible in Sections 2-3 and Exit Check

constraints: Scale of 10 only throughout entire module — no mixing with scales 2 or 5
Bar graph interaction is click-to-set — NOT drag
'Tick marks' vocabulary FORBIDDEN — use 'the lines' or 'marks on the axis' (conflicts with Fractions unit)
Ghost gridlines at 5s must be present for all interpolation tasks
Values in Section 1 are multiples of 10 only; Sections 2-3 include multiples of 5
Interpolation limited to halfway points only (multiples of 5) — no values like 37 or 43
Picture graphs appear ONLY for comparison in Warmup — students do NOT create them
Pre-fill progression: 3→2+→0 pre-filled bars across sections
Horizontal orientation introduced in Section 3 with Guide acknowledgment

advanced_vocabulary: tick marks (conflicts with Fractions unit — use 'the lines' or 'marks on the axis')
interpolate / estimate (students DO it, don't formally name it)
scale of 2 or 5 (avoid mixing — save for M5 comparison)
advanced_concepts: Picture graph CREATION with scale of 10 (observation only in M4)
Two-step problems as single submissions (Module 6 deep practice)
Values at non-halfway points (e.g., 37 between 30-40 — too complex for M4)
required_phrases: The HEIGHT OF THE BAR shows...

forbidden_phrases.reason: Conflicts with Fractions unit number lines — use 'the lines' or 'marks on the axis'
Wrong scales — M4 is scale 10 only
Avoid mixing scales — M4 uses scale of 10 only

forbidden_phrases.phrase: You're so smart! / Perfect! / Amazing! / Excellent!

misconceptions.description: When a bar falls between 30 and 40 on an axis scaled by 10s, student reports '30' or 'I don't know' instead of reading 35. Student reads only values at the lines, unable to determine values between them.
why_it_happens: M2-M3 used values only at axis marks (no interpolation required)
Natural tendency to read only labeled numbers
'Halfway' concept not yet connected to graph reading
May not realize bars can represent values not shown on axis
prevention: Explicit halfway teaching in 2.1: 'This bar ends BETWEEN THE LINES—between 30 and 40. What's halfway? Half of 10 is 5. So 30 + 5 = 35.'
Half-symbol bridge: brief callback to M2: 'Remember half-symbols? Same idea—half of 10 is 5.'
Ghost gridlines at 5s help students see halfway points
Multiple practice opportunities before Exit Check

remediation.light: Look where the bar ends. Is it at a line, or between two lines? What's halfway between those?
remediation.medium: Highlight the two axis marks. '30... 40. What's in the middle? Half of 10 is 5, so 30 + 5 = ?'
remediation.heavy: Count up from lower mark: 'Start at 30. Count by 5s: 30... 35... 40. Where does the bar stop?'

misconceptions.description: During transformation animation or when reading picture graphs, student counts symbols directly instead of using scale. Less common in M4 because bar graphs are primary.
why_it_happens: Discrete symbols feel 'countable' in a way bars don't
May not attend to key/scale during transformation
prevention: Transformation narration: Guide explicitly counts by 10s during animation: 'Watch—5 symbols, each worth 10. That's 10, 20, 30, 40, 50.'
Immediate bar connection: 'The bar shows the same thing—50—without counting symbols.'
Limited picture graph exposure: students don't CREATE picture graphs

remediation.light: Check the key. Each symbol = 10. Count by 10s, not by 1s.
remediation.medium: Return to comparison: bar graph vs picture graph for same value
remediation.heavy: N/A — M4 keeps picture graph exposure minimal

misconceptions.description: Student treats graph as literal drawing rather than data representation. May expect bar heights to visually 'look like' what they represent.
why_it_happens: Transition from picture graphs (which DO 'look like' data) to bars (abstract)
Continuous bars feel less connected to discrete items
Young students may expect visual realism
prevention: Transformation animation: explicitly shows picture → bar, reinforcing bars REPRESENT the same data
Guide language: 'The bar shows 50 votes. You can't SEE 50 things, but the HEIGHT tells you the amount.'
Consistent abstraction: all M4 work uses bars

remediation.light: The bar's HEIGHT shows the amount. Where does it end on the axis?
remediation.medium: Show side-by-side: picture graph vs bar graph same data

phases.purpose: Activate prior knowledge of scaled bar graphs (1:5) from M3 while introducing scale of 10 as 'even more efficient.' Students collect personal data with scale-10 values, see BOTH a 1:10 picture graph (their only exposure to this format) AND a 1:10 bar graph. Discovery: 'scale of 10 uses even fewer symbols' creates motivation for Lesson's bar graph creation focus. Key difference from M3: picture-to-bar bridge added; no grouping animation.

variables.format: Data Collection Game → Scale Comparison (1:5 vs 1:10) → Picture-to-Bar Bridge → Lesson Bridge

interactions: W.1: Data Collection Game — Mario Party-style counting, 3-4 categories, multiples of 10 only (20-60 range)
W.2: Scale Comparison (1:5 vs 1:10) — same data as both picture graphs side-by-side; MC: 'Which uses FEWER symbols?'; establishes scale-of-10 efficiency
W.3: Picture-to-Bar Bridge — 1:10 picture graph alongside 1:10 bar graph (same data); student identifies matching category; 'This is student's ONLY exposure to 1:10 picture graphs'
W.4: Bridge to Lesson — bar graph remains, picture graph removed; 'Ready to learn how to CREATE bar graphs with scale of 10?'

phases.purpose: Establish scale of 10 through skip-counting by 10s. Students read and create bar graphs where all values are multiples of 10. Formal 'axis' vocabulary introduced. Build on Warmup's data collection. Worked example (1.1) shows bar reading before students attempt creation.
interactions: 1.1: Reading Bar Heights [WORKED EXAMPLE] — Warmup data bar graph; Guide narrates 'height of the bar' and 'axis'; no student prompt
1.2: Reading Bar Height — MC: Blue bar at 30; distractors include '3' (symbol count) and '35' (between-lines error)
1.3: Skip-Counting Connection — Guide counts by 10s for Green (50); student reads Yellow (20); MC: 2, 12, 20, 25
1.4: Your Turn — Create a Bar — 'Favorite Recess Activities'; 3 of 4 pre-filled; student sets Swings at 30
1.5: Create Another Bar — 'Books Read This Month'; 3 of 4 pre-filled; student sets Nonfiction at 60
1.6: Reading for Comparison — completed graph; data table hidden; 'How many MORE Nonfiction than Comics?'

phases.purpose: Introduce interpolation — reading and creating values BETWEEN axis lines. Students learn bars can end 'between the lines' to show values like 15, 25, 35, 45. Connect to 'half of 10 = 5' principle with brief half-symbol callback from M2. Ghost gridlines at 5s provide visual scaffolding.

variables.value_range: 15-55 (multiples of 5, mix of at-line and between-line)

interactions: 2.1: Discovering Between-the-Lines [WORKED EXAMPLE] — 'Stickers Collected'; Jake's bar at 25 (between 20-30); Guide teaches 'half of 10 = 5'; brief M2 half-symbol callback; no student prompt
2.2: Reading Between the Lines — Leo's bar at 35 (between 30-40); MC: 30, 34, 35, 40 (Misconception #5 distractors)
2.3: Creating a Between-the-Lines Bar — 'Points Scored'; 4 of 5 pre-filled; student sets Team A at 45 (between 40-50)
2.4: Create Another Between-the-Lines Bar — 'Laps Completed'; 3 of 4 pre-filled; student sets Runner C at 35
2.5: Mixed Values — At Lines AND Between — 'Minutes of Reading'; 0 pre-filled; all 4 bars (2 at lines, 2 between)
2.6: Reading Mixed Values — completed graph; data table hidden; 'Total minutes for Monday and Tuesday?'

phases.purpose: Full independence — students create bar graphs with scale of 10 including values at AND between axis lines. Horizontal orientation introduced. No pre-filled bars. Guide explicitly acknowledges orientation switch.

variables.value_range: 20-60 (multiples of 5, mix of at-line and between-line)

interactions: 3.1: Horizontal Orientation Introduction — 'Votes for Field Trip' (5 categories); Guide acknowledges horizontal; 'What value does the Museum bar show?' MC: 3, 25, 30, 35
3.2: Reading Horizontal — Between the Lines — Park bar at 55; MC: 50, 55, 58, 60
3.3: Create Horizontal Bar Graph — Full Independence — 'Miles Walked'; 0 pre-filled; all 4 bars (mix of at-line and between-line)
3.4: Comparison from Horizontal Graph — 'How many MORE miles did Ava walk than Noah?' (50 vs 25); data table hidden
3.5: Final Challenge — Create and Analyze — Part 1: create all 4 bars from table; Part 2: table hides, 'How many plants IN ALL for Peppers and Carrots?' (35+45)

phases.purpose: Verify Lesson understanding before Practice. Tests reading at axis lines, reading between lines (interpolation), creating bars with mixed values, and comparison problem-solving. First module to use all three cognitive types: CREATE, IDENTIFY, and COMPARE.

graph_types: bar graph only (picture graphs not used)

variables.value_range: 20-60 (multiples of 5, mix of at-line and between-line)

interactions: EC.1: Read Value at Axis Line (IDENTIFY) — 'Books Checked Out'; Fiction bar at 40; MC: 4, 35, 40, 45; tests Lesson 1.2-1.3
EC.2: Read Value Between Lines — Interpolation (IDENTIFY) — 'Points Earned'; Team A at 35 (between 30-40); MC: 30, 34, 35, 40; tests Lesson 2.1-2.2
EC.3: Create Bar Graph with Mixed Values (CREATE) — 'Art Supplies Collected'; 0 pre-filled; 4 bars (2 at lines, 2 between); data table visible; tests Lesson 1.4-1.5 and 2.3-2.5
EC.4: Comparison Problem (COMPARE) — EC.3 graph remains; data table hidden; 'How many MORE Glue Sticks than Markers?' (55-30=25); tests Lesson 3.4-3.5

phases.purpose: Consolidate scale of 10 as part of the scale progression (2→5→10). Connect interpolation principle to mathematical reasoning. Reflect on how the axis helps read both exact AND between-the-lines values. 4 interactions: scale progression pattern, orientation transfer, real-world connection, metacognitive reflection on 'half of 10 = 5' strategy.
interactions: S.1: Scale Progression Pattern — four bar graphs side-by-side (same data at scales 1, 2, 5, 10); click which uses fewest lines; 'You've learned four scales now: 1, 2, 5, and 10'
S.2: Orientation Transfer — same data as vertical and horizontal bar graphs; click the 35 bar in both; confirms orientation doesn't change math
S.3: Real-World Connection — multi-select: money (dollar stacks), ruler (cm), counting one-by-one; correct: money and ruler; 'counting by 10s shows up everywhere'

S.4: Metacognitive Reflection + Closure — 'What strategy helped find values between the lines?'; MC includes 'I found halfway—that's always 5 more'; Bridge: 'Next time, you'll pick the best scale for any data' (M5 preview)
